unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 8! This unit is all about homes. You will learn the names of homes for people and for animals — a house, an apartment, a hut, a tree house, a nest, a hive and a hole. You will name the rooms inside a home and the things we keep in them, and you will compare homes from around the world. You will say where things are with in, on, under, next to, between and above, use there is and there are to say what a home has, and use will to promise the jobs you will do at home. By the end you can give a tour of your own home, just like Amal does in this unit's story, “Helping Hands at Home”.
unit.learningPath:
Watch the teacher lecture, then learn a few vocabulary words each day and name three real things around you.
Work through the six grammar practices: prepositions of place, there is and there are, and talking about the future with will.
Read the poem “Homes”, the text “Different Kinds of Homes”, Theo's listening text and the story “Helping Hands at Home”, and answer the nineteen comprehension questions.
Complete the six speaking, six writing and six activity practices.
Join three live teacher sessions in each of the two weeks.
Finish with the ten-question checkpoint and the self-assessment.
unit.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 2 Unit 8 source pack under docs/lesson-authoring-super-prompt.md
unit.reviewStatus: Pilot rebuild - pending curriculum reviewer sign-off

visual.alt: Cartoon cut-away of a family home showing a bedroom, kitchen, bathroom and living room, with a tree house in the garden.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A building where a family lives, with walls, a roof and rooms.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My family lives in a house with a small garden.
practiceSentences: Amal lives in a house near the busy market.
Is your house next to the school or the park?
“Our house feels warm when the lamps are on,” said Nora.
I help to sweep our house every Saturday morning.
The old house on the hill has a bright red roof.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: h - o - u - s - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Sit with your helper and ask the tutor to listen while you describe your house in three sentences.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A set of rooms to live in inside a bigger building.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: In the city, many people live in an apartment.
practiceSentences: Leo lives in an apartment on the fifth floor.
Does your apartment have a balcony?
“Our apartment is small but very cosy,” said Maya.
I water the plants in our apartment every evening.
The apartment building has ten floors and two lifts.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - p - a - r - t - m - e - n - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside you, ask the tutor to explain how an apartment is different from a house.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A small, simple house with only one or two rooms.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Some people live in a hut made of mud and straw.
practiceSentences: The little hut has one door and one window.
Is the hut made of mud, wood or straw?
“We rest in the hut when the sun is hot,” said Omar.
I drew a hut with a pointed grass roof.
The hut stays cool because its walls are thick.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: h - u - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask your helper to listen while the tutor tells you what a hut is usually built from.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A small house built up in the branches of a tree.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The children climb a ladder to their tree house.
practiceSentences: Theo and Sami read their comics in the tree house.
Does your tree house have a rope ladder?
“Our tree house has a tiny window,” said Theo.
I would love a tree house in our garden.
The tree house sits in the branches of the old tree.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: t - r - e - e / h - o - u - s - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor to describe a tree house while an adult listens, then draw the one you heard.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A home that a bird builds to keep its eggs safe.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The bird made a nest in the tall tree.
practiceSentences: Three small eggs lie in the soft nest.
Which bird built this neat little nest?
“Look, there is a nest above our window!” cried Mina.
I watched a nest from my bedroom window all week.
The nest is made of twigs, grass and feathers.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: n - e - s - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With your helper next to you, ask the tutor which other animals build a nest.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A home where bees live together and make their honey.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The bees make honey in their hive.
practiceSentences: The hive hums all day with busy bees.
How many bees can live in one hive?
“Stand well back from the hive,” said Father kindly.
I have never looked inside a real hive.
Bees fly out of the hive to find flowers.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: h - i - v - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor how bees build a hive, then retell the answer to the adult sitting with you.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: An opening in the ground where some small animals live.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The rabbit ran into its hole.
practiceSentences: A rabbit digs a deep hole under the hedge.
Which animal lives in that hole by the tree?
“A mouse ran into the hole!” shouted Idris.
I found a small hole between the tree roots.
The hole keeps the rabbit warm and safe.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: h - o - l - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor to name three animals that live in a hole, and check the list with your helper.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A room where people sleep and keep their clothes.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I keep my bed and my toys in my bedroom.
practiceSentences: Amal shares a bedroom with her little brother Idris.
Is your bedroom upstairs or downstairs?
“My bedroom window faces the garden,” said Nora.
I tidy my bedroom before I go out to play.
The bedroom has a soft bed and a small closet.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: b - e - d - r - o - o - m
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Tell the tutor three things in your bedroom and ask it to spell each word back to you, with an adult listening.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A room where food is cooked and dishes are washed.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Mother is cooking rice in the kitchen.
practiceSentences: The kitchen smells of warm bread every morning.
Is anyone in the kitchen making breakfast?
“Please carry the plates to the kitchen,” said Mother.
I help to wash the dishes in the kitchen sink.
Our kitchen has a small table and four chairs.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: k - i - t - c - h - e - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor to name five things found in a kitchen, then point to the ones in your own kitchen with an adult.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A room with a sink and a toilet, used for washing.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I wash my hands in the bathroom.
practiceSentences: The bathroom has a big white sink and a mirror.
May I use the bathroom, please?
“The bathroom mirror is all steamy,” laughed Idris.
I brush my teeth in the bathroom every night.
Our bathroom is next to the bedroom.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: b - a - t - h - r - o - o - m
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Say the word bathroom in two beats for the tutor and ask it to check your syllables while your helper watches.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A room where a family sits together, talks and rests.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We drink tea together in the living room.
practiceSentences: A bright red rug lies on the living room floor.
Shall we read our books in the living room?
“Grandma is resting in the living room,” whispered Amal.
I like the living room best because we all sit there.
The living room has a long sofa and two chairs.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: l - i - v - i - n - g / r - o - o - m
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor what families usually do in a living room, then tell your helper what your family does there.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A room where people sit at a table to eat meals.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We eat dinner in the dining room.
practiceSentences: The dining room table is long enough for eight people.
Do you eat in the dining room or in the kitchen?
“Please set the dining room table,” said Father.
I put fresh flowers in the dining room today.
Our dining room is between the kitchen and the living room.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: d - i - n - i - n - g / r - o - o - m
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor to explain the difference between a dining room and a living room while an adult sits with you.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A piece of furniture that you lie on to sleep.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: There is a soft bed in my bedroom.
practiceSentences: Amal pulled the blanket flat across her bed.
Is your bed next to the window?
“My bed is the cosiest place in the house,” said Mina.
I keep a small lamp beside my bed.
The toy box sits under the bed.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: b - e - d
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Describe your bed to the tutor in one sentence and ask it to add one describing word, with your helper listening.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A piece of furniture with a flat top and legs.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We put the food on the table.
practiceSentences: Idris wiped the little table until the dust was gone.
Is the lamp on the table or on the shelf?
“Put the plates on the table, please,” said Mother.
I do my homework at the kitchen table.
The table stands between two wooden chairs.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: t - a - b - l - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor to say three things that are on a table at dinner time, and check them with an adult.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A seat with a back and legs, made for one person.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Please sit on the chair.
practiceSentences: Grandma Hana rested in the big soft chair.
Is that chair next to the window free?
“Pull your chair closer to the table,” said Father.
I hang my school bag on the back of my chair.
The chair by the door has a red cushion.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - h - a - i - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Count the chairs in your home, tell the tutor the number, and ask it to make a sentence using there are.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A long, soft seat made for two or three people.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We sit on the sofa to listen to stories.
practiceSentences: Idris found his lost toys under the sofa.
Would you like to sit on the sofa with me?
“Our sofa is old but very comfortable,” said Nora.
I curl up on the sofa with a book after school.
The sofa faces the window in the living room.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - o - f - a
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor how a sofa is different from a chair, then say the answer in your own words to your helper.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A basin with a tap where you wash things with water.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I put the dirty dishes in the sink.
practiceSentences: Amal washed every plate in the kitchen sink.
Is there hot water in the bathroom sink?
“Leave the cups beside the sink,” said Mother.
I rinse my paintbrush under the sink tap.
The sink is full of warm, soapy water.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - i - n - k
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Tell the tutor which two rooms in a home have a sink, and ask an adult to check your answer.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A soft mat that covers part of the floor.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: There is a bright rug on the floor.
practiceSentences: Amal shook the red rug outside until it was clean.
Is the rug in front of the sofa?
“Wipe your feet before you step on the rug,” said Father.
I sit on the rug to build my tower of blocks.
The rug feels warm and soft under my bare feet.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: r - u - g
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Describe the colour and shape of a rug to the tutor and ask it to repeat your description, with an adult present.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: An opening in a wall with glass that lets light in.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: There are two windows in my room.
practiceSentences: Idris looked through the window at the big tree.
Is your window open or closed tonight?
“Look outside the window!” called Father.
I can see the market from my bedroom window.
The window above the sofa lets in the morning sun.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: w - i - n - d - o - w
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Count the windows in one room, tell the tutor the number, and ask an adult to help you check it.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To pull the sheets flat and tidy the cover on your bed.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Every morning I make my bed before breakfast.
practiceSentences: I make my bed as soon as I get up.
Today I will make my bed without any help.
“I make my bed first, then I tidy my toys,” said Amal.
Can you show me how to make my bed neatly?
It takes me only two minutes to make my bed.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - a - k - e / m - y / b - e - d
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Tell the tutor the steps you follow to make my bed, in order, while your helper listens.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To put every toy, book and piece of clothing back in its place.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I tidy my room before I play outside.
practiceSentences: On Saturday I tidy my room from top to bottom.
Will you help me tidy my room today?
“I tidy my room every evening,” said Nora proudly.
I tidy my room so that I can find my books.
It is easier to tidy my room with music playing.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: t - i - d - y / m - y / r - o - o - m
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor for three tips to tidy my room quickly, then try one of them with an adult.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To clean the floor by brushing the dust away with a broom.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Idris helps to sweep the floor after dinner.
practiceSentences: Amal took the big broom to sweep the floor.
Who will sweep the floor this evening?
“Please sweep the floor before Grandma arrives,” said Mother.
I sweep the floor every Saturday morning.
Tomorrow we will sweep the floor and shake the rug.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - w - e - e - p / t - h - e / f - l - o - o - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Act out sweeping while you say sweep the floor, and ask the tutor to check that your words match the action.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To put the plates, cups and spoons ready for a meal.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I set the table before dinner.
practiceSentences: Amal helped to set the table with clean plates and cups.
Will you set the table for six people?
“Please set the table now,” called Father from the kitchen.
I set the table while the rice is cooking.
Tonight Mina will set the table instead of me.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - e - t / t - h - e / t - a - b - l - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: List for the tutor everything you need to set the table for four people, and check the list with an adult.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To clean the plates, cups and pots in water.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: After we eat, I wash the dishes.
practiceSentences: Amal and Idris wash the dishes together after dinner.
Who will wash the dishes tonight?
“I will wash the dishes if you dry them,” said Adam.
I wash the dishes in warm, soapy water.
We wash the dishes before we go out to play.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: w - a - s - h / t - h - e / d - i - s - h - e - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Tell the tutor who helps you wash the dishes at home and ask it to turn your answer into a will sentence.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A house built from mud and dry grass, with thick, cool walls.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: In hot, dry places there are adobe houses.
practiceSentences: The thick walls of an adobe house keep it cool.
Have you ever seen an adobe house?
“An adobe house is built from mud and grass,” explained our teacher.
I would like to visit an adobe house one day.
An adobe house stays cool even at midday.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - d - o - b - e / h - o - u - s - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor why an adobe house has such thick walls, then explain the reason to your helper.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A house built high on wooden poles above water or wet ground.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The stilt house stays cool as air blows under it.
practiceSentences: A stilt house stands on tall wooden poles.
Why is a stilt house built so high?
“The water never reaches our stilt house,” said the boat builder.
I climbed the steps up to the stilt house.
The floor of the stilt house is high above the river.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - t - i - l - t / h - o - u - s - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor what happens under a stilt house when it rains hard, and talk about it with an adult.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A home that people dig into the rock of a cliff.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: A cave house is cool in summer and warm in winter.
practiceSentences: People cut a cave house straight into the soft rock.
Would you like to sleep in a cave house?
“A cave house never gets too hot,” said our guide.
I saw a picture of a cave house in my book.
The rock walls of a cave house keep the rooms cool.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - a - v - e / h - o - u - s - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor to compare a cave house with your own home, then tell an adult one difference you heard.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A very tall building in a city, with many floors.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: That skyscraper has more than a hundred floors!
practiceSentences: The skyscraper is the tallest building in the whole city.
How many lifts does a skyscraper need?
“I can see the whole city from this skyscraper,” said Maya.
I counted the shiny windows on the skyscraper.
A skyscraper is far taller than our little house.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - k - y - s - c - r - a - p - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor how many floors a very tall skyscraper can have, and say the number back to your helper.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Name different kinds of homes for people and animals.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Six homes matched to who or what lives in them in Activity 1, and one home described in full in the Speaking 1 recording.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Name the rooms in a home and say what people do in each one.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Five rooms matched to their activities in Activity 2, and three rooms described aloud in Speaking 2.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Name common things at home and say which room they belong in.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Twelve household objects sorted under the right room heading in Activity 5, and the three object questions answered correctly in the checkpoint quiz.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Compare different kinds of homes around the world.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: One likeness and two differences written about two world homes in Writing 5, plus the adobe and stilt answers in comprehension questions 6 and 7.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Use prepositions of place (in, on, under, next to, between, above) to say where things are.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: All five prepositions correct in Activity 3, and six different place words used correctly on the labelled home map in Activity 6.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Use there is and there are to say what a home has.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Five correct is or are choices in Grammar Practice 3, and six accurate There is and There are sentences in Writing 4.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Talk about the future with will.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Four future sentences rewritten correctly in Grammar Practice 5, and six dated promises using will and will not in Writing 6.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Describe your own home in speaking and writing.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: A paragraph of four to six sentences about the learner's own home in Writing 1, and a room-by-room tour in the Speaking 3 recording.

readings.title: A Poem: “Homes”
readings.passageScript:
Read this little poem out loud:
A nest is a home for a bird.
A hive is a home for a bee.
A hole is a home for a rabbit.
And a house is a home for me.

readings.title: Different Kinds of Homes
readings.passageScript:
Read this text:
People live in many different kinds of homes around the world. In cities, many people live in apartments. An apartment is a set of rooms that is part of a big building. Some people live in houses with a garden. In some hot, dry places, people build houses from mud and dry grass, called adobe houses. The thick mud walls keep them cool. In some hot, wet places, people build houses high on stilts so the air can blow underneath. Every home is special because it keeps people safe, warm and dry.

readings.title: Theo's Tree House
readings.passageScript:
Listen to Theo. (Ask your AI tutor to read it aloud, then read it yourself.)
“My name is Theo. My father built a tree house in our garden. It is like a small house in a tree! It has walls, a roof and a small window. To get up, you climb a ladder. The tree house has two floors. I love playing there with my friends.”

readings.title: Helping Hands at Home
readings.theme: Rooms in a home, things at home, and helping your family.
readings.setting: A family home near the town market, on the day before Grandma's visit.
readings.passageScript:
Amal lived in a small house near the market with her mother, her father, and her little brother Idris. The house had two bedrooms, a kitchen, a bathroom, and a cosy living room with a bright red rug on the floor.
One sunny morning, Mother had good news. “Grandma Hana will visit us tomorrow,” she said. “She will stay for one whole week!”
“Grandma is coming!” shouted Idris, jumping up and down.
Amal smiled, but then she looked around. There were toys under the sofa. There were dishes in the sink. There was dust on the little table by the window. The house was not ready for a special guest.
“Our home must look nice for Grandma,” said Mother. “But I cannot do it all by myself. Will you help me?”
“I will help!” said Amal.
“Me too!” said Idris.
Mother clapped her hands happily. “Then we are a team,” she said. “Let us start.”
First, they went to the bedroom. “I will make my bed,” said Amal. She pulled the blanket flat and put her pillow at the top. Then she put her clean clothes away in the closet. Idris picked up his toys from under the bed and put them in the toy box.
“Look, Amal!” he said. “Now the floor is clear!”
Next, they went to the living room. Amal swept the floor with the big broom. She shook the red rug outside so that it was clean again. Idris wiped the little table until the dust was gone.
“Where should I put this book?” asked Idris.
“Put it on the shelf, next to the other books,” said Amal. Idris placed it neatly on the shelf.
In the kitchen, Mother was cooking sweet rice and warm vegetable pies for tomorrow. The kitchen smelled wonderful. Amal washed the dishes in the sink, and Idris dried them with a cloth. Then Amal set the table with clean plates and cups.
“You are working so hard,” said Father, coming in from the garden. “I have news too. Look outside the window!”
The children ran to the window. In the garden, under the big tree, Father had built something new. It had walls, a small roof, and a tiny window.
“A tree house!” cried Amal. “For us?”
“Yes,” laughed Father. “When your work is done, you may climb up and play. But first, we finish helping your mother.”
So Amal and Idris worked until every room was tidy. The bedroom was neat. The living room was clean. There were fresh flowers on the table, and the whole house felt warm and welcoming.
The next day, Grandma Hana arrived. She looked around the little house and smiled a big smile.
“What a beautiful home,” she said. “It is clean, it is warm, and it is full of love. Who helped to make it so lovely?”
“We did!” said Amal and Idris together.
Grandma Hana hugged them both. “A home is not just walls and a roof,” she said softly. “A home is a place where a family helps each other. Thank you, my dear helping hands.”
That evening, Amal and Idris climbed the ladder to their new tree house. They looked down at their little house, warm and bright below them.
“Home, sweet home,” said Amal happily. And she felt very glad that she had helped.

comprehension.question: Which three animal homes does the poem name, and which animal lives in each one?
comprehension.correctAnswer: A nest is for a bird, a hive is for a bee, and a hole is for a rabbit.
comprehension.explanation: Lines one, two and three each name one animal and its home, so read them in order to collect all three pairs.

comprehension.question: In the last line of the poem, what kind of home does the speaker live in?
comprehension.explanation: The final line says, “And a house is a home for me.”, so the speaker's own home is a house.

comprehension.question: The poem repeats the same sentence pattern in every line. Write that pattern with two blanks.
comprehension.correctAnswer: A ______ is a home for a ______.
comprehension.explanation: Poems often repeat a pattern. Here the home comes first and the animal comes last in every line, which makes the poem easy to join in with.

comprehension.question: Name two kinds of homes that the text says people live in.
comprehension.correctAnswer: Any two of: apartments, houses with a garden, adobe houses, houses on stilts.
comprehension.explanation: The text names four kinds of home in turn. Two of them are enough for the mark.

comprehension.question: The text says many people in cities live in apartments. What exactly is an apartment?
comprehension.correctAnswer: A set of rooms that is part of a big building.
comprehension.explanation: The second and third sentences give the meaning of the word apartment right after they use it.

comprehension.question: What are adobe houses built from, and why do their thick walls help in a hot, dry place?
comprehension.correctAnswer: They are built from mud and dry grass, and the thick mud walls keep the rooms cool.
comprehension.explanation: Both halves of the answer sit in the same two sentences: one gives the material and the next gives the reason.

comprehension.question: In hot, wet places, why do people build their houses high on stilts?
comprehension.correctAnswer: So that the air can blow underneath the house.
comprehension.explanation: The text gives the reason straight after the fact, using the word so, which signals why something is done.

comprehension.question: Who built Theo's tree house, and where did he build it?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Theo's father built it, and it is in their garden.
comprehension.explanation: Theo says it in his second sentence, naming both the builder and the place.

comprehension.question: How does Theo get up into his tree house?
comprehension.explanation: Theo explains, “To get up, you climb a ladder.”, so a ladder is the only way up.

comprehension.question: Theo says his tree house is like a small house in a tree. Which three parts make it like a house?
comprehension.correctAnswer: It has walls, a roof and a small window.
comprehension.explanation: Theo lists the three parts together, and they are the same parts that any house has.

comprehension.question: Would you like to have a tree house of your own? Give one reason for your answer.
comprehension.correctAnswer: Any clear yes or no answer with one sensible reason, such as “Yes, because I could read up in the branches.”
comprehension.explanation: The mark is for the reason, not for the choice. Look for a full sentence that joins the opinion to a because.

comprehension.question: Who is coming to visit Amal's family, and how long will she stay?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Grandma Hana is coming, and she will stay for one whole week.
comprehension.explanation: Mother's good news at the start of the story names the visitor and the length of the visit.

comprehension.question: Name three rooms in Amal's house.
comprehension.correctAnswer: Any three of: the two bedrooms, the kitchen, the bathroom, the living room.
comprehension.explanation: The very first paragraph lists every room in the house before the story begins.

comprehension.question: In the bedroom, what job did Amal do and what job did Idris do?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Amal made her bed and put her clean clothes in the closet. Idris picked up his toys from under the bed and put them in the toy box.
comprehension.explanation: Both jobs are in the paragraph that begins “First, they went to the bedroom.”, one after the other.

comprehension.question: What had Father built in the garden as a surprise, and what three things does it have?
comprehension.correctAnswer: A tree house, with walls, a small roof and a tiny window.
comprehension.explanation: The children see it through the window, and the narrator lists its three parts before Amal names it.

comprehension.question: What did Grandma Hana say a home really is?
comprehension.correctAnswer: A place where a family helps each other, not just walls and a roof.
comprehension.explanation: Grandma Hana says this after she hugs the children, and it is the lesson of the whole story.

comprehension.question: Why did the house need to be tidied before Grandma Hana came? Give one detail from the story.
comprehension.correctAnswer: The house was not ready for a special guest: there were toys under the sofa, dishes in the sink and dust on the little table.
comprehension.explanation: Amal notices three untidy things in one paragraph, so any one of them proves the point.

comprehension.question: Grandma Hana called Amal and Idris her “helping hands”. Which two jobs do you do at home to be a helping hand?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Any two real jobs named in full sentences, such as “At home, I set the table.” and “At home, I sweep the floor.”
comprehension.explanation: Award the mark when both jobs are real household jobs and each is written as a complete sentence.

comprehension.question: How did Amal feel at the end of the story, and why do you think helping made her feel that way?
comprehension.correctAnswer: She felt very glad. Accept any reason grounded in the story, such as the house looked lovely, Grandma was happy, or the family worked as a team.
comprehension.explanation: The last sentence gives the feeling. The second mark is for a reason that links her gladness to the helping she did.

grammar.title: Prepositions of Place
grammar.explanation: Prepositions of place are small words that tell us where something is. They help you describe your home so that another person can picture every room.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
in means inside something: The bed is in the bedroom.
on means touching the top of something: The book is on the table.
under means below something: The shoes are under the bed.
next to means beside something: The chair is next to the window.
between means in the middle of two things: The lamp is between the two books.
above means higher than something: The picture is above the sofa.
grammar.commonMistake: Children often write “The book is in the table.” The book is touching the top and not sitting inside, so the right word is on: “The book is on the table.”
grammar.memoryTip: Point as you say it. Put your hand in a box, on a box and under a box, and your hand will help your brain remember each word.
grammar.practice:
Fill in each blank with in, on, under, next to, between or above.
1. The rug is ______ the floor.
2. The clothes are ______ the closet.
3. The cat is hiding ______ the chair.
4. The window is ______ the bed, higher up on the wall.
5. The little table is ______ the two beds.

grammar.title: Prepositions of Place: Describe Your Own Room
grammar.explanation: Now you choose the place words yourself. When you describe your own room, every preposition has to match exactly where the object really is.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Start with the object, then the place word, then the second object.
My schoolbag is under my bed.
The lamp is next to my pillow.
The family photograph hangs above my desk.
My shoes are in the closet.
grammar.commonMistake: It is easy to use next to for everything. Save next to for things that stand side by side, and use between only when there is one thing on each side.
grammar.memoryTip: Say each sentence while you stand in the room and point at the object. If your finger and your words disagree, change the place word.
grammar.practice: Stand in your bedroom or your living room. Write six sentences about where things really are, using a different place word in each one: in, on, under, next to, between and above. Read them to an adult and check each one by pointing.

grammar.title: There is / There are
grammar.explanation: We use there is and there are to say what can be found in a place. This is the perfect way to tell somebody what your home has in each room.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use there is with one thing.
There is a bed in the bedroom.
There is a kitchen in my house.
There is a bird in the nest.
Use there are with more than one thing.
There are two windows in my room.
There are three bedrooms in the apartment.
There are books on the shelf.
grammar.commonMistake: Children often write “There is two chairs in the kitchen.” Two chairs is more than one, so the sentence needs there are: “There are two chairs in the kitchen.”
grammar.memoryTip: Listen for the s at the end of the thing you are counting. Chairs, beds and windows all end in s, so they take there are.
grammar.practice:
Write is or are in each gap.
1. There ______ a sofa in the living room.
2. There ______ four chairs at the table.
3. There ______ a rug on the floor.
4. There ______ two windows in the kitchen.
5. There ______ a toilet in the bathroom.

grammar.title: There is / There are: What My Home Has
grammar.explanation: Now you describe your own home. Count the things first, then choose there is for one thing and there are for two or more.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Name the room, then say what it has.
In my kitchen there is one big table.
In my kitchen there are six chairs.
In my bedroom there is a warm blanket.
In my bedroom there are three books on the shelf.
grammar.commonMistake: A list can hide the number. In “There is a sink and a mirror.” the writer has counted two things, so the sentence should begin There are.
grammar.memoryTip: Count on your fingers before you speak. One finger means there is, and two or more fingers mean there are.
grammar.practice: Choose three rooms in your home. Write two sentences about each room: one that begins There is and one that begins There are. Underline the thing you counted in every sentence.

grammar.title: Talking About the Future with “will”
grammar.explanation: We use will to talk about something that has not happened yet. In this unit you use will to promise the jobs you are going to do at home.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use will and then the plain verb. The word will never changes.
I will tidy my room after lunch.
We will build a tree house in the garden.
She will help her mother cook.
To say no, use will not. Its short form is won't.
I will not forget to make my bed.
It won't rain today.
grammar.commonMistake: Children often write “I will to help my father.” The word to is not needed after will, so the sentence is “I will help my father.”
grammar.memoryTip: Think of will as a promise word. Say will, then say the action word on its own: will sweep, will wash, will set.
grammar.practice:
Rewrite each sentence so that it talks about the future. Use will and the verb in brackets.
1. Tomorrow I ______ (sweep) the floor.
2. After dinner we ______ (wash) the dishes.
3. She ______ (set) the table tonight.
4. They ______ (build) a tree house in the garden.

grammar.title: Talking About the Future with “will”: My Promises
grammar.explanation: Now you make your own promises about later today, tomorrow and next week. A promise sentence names the job and says when you will do it.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Say when, then who, then will and the plain verb.
After school I will set the table.
Tomorrow Idris will sweep the floor.
On Saturday we will wash the dishes together.
Tonight I will not leave my toys on the rug.
grammar.commonMistake: Adding -ing after will is a common slip, as in “I will sweeping the floor.” Keep the verb plain: “I will sweep the floor.”
grammar.memoryTip: Read your promise aloud and take the word will away. If what is left still sounds like a whole action, your sentence is right.
grammar.practice: Write five promises about helping at home this week. Use will in four of them and will not in one. Say when you will do each job, then read your list to an adult and tick every promise when it is done.

speaking.title: Speaking 1 — Describe a Kind of Home
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Choose one home from your word list: a house, an apartment, a hut, a tree house, a nest, a hive or a hole. With an adult listening, say four sentences about it.
“This is a ______.” or “This is an ______.”
“It is made of ______.”
“______ live in this home.”
“It has ______.”
Record yourself. Check: did you name the home, say what it is made of, and say who lives there?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor to choose a home from the list while your helper listens, then describe that home in four sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 2 — Describe a Room
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Pick three rooms in a home: bedroom, kitchen, bathroom, living room or dining room. For each room say three sentences.
“This is the ______.”
“People ______ in this room.”
“In this room there is ______.” or “In this room there are ______.”
Ask an adult to listen and to tell you which of your three room descriptions was the clearest.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With your helper beside you, ask the tutor to name a room and to check whether you chose there is or there are correctly.

speaking.title: Speaking Game — My Home Tour
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Give a tour of your home to your family or, with an adult beside you, to your AI tutor. Walk from room to room and say “This is my ______.” Then say what you do there: “In this room, we ______.” Then say what is inside: “There is ______.” or “There are ______.” Finish with your favourite room: “I like this room because ______.”
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Give your whole home tour to the tutor while an adult sits with you, then ask for one idea that would make the tour more interesting.

speaking.title: Speaking 4 — Homes Around the World
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Choose two homes from Group 5: an adobe house, a stilt house, a cave house or a skyscraper. Say one way the two homes are the same and two ways they are different. Then explain why one of them suits its weather, for example “An adobe house has thick walls, so it stays cool.” Record your talk and play it back to an adult.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor why adobe houses have such thick walls, then say the reason again in your own words while an adult listens.

speaking.title: Speaking 5 — Where Does It Belong?
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Choose six things in one room, but do not tell your partner what they are. Describe where each thing is, using in, on, under, next to, between and above once each, for example “It is under the bed.” Your partner has to guess the object every time. Then swap over and guess your partner's six things.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Describe where three things are in your room and ask the tutor to guess each object, with an adult listening in.

speaking.title: Speaking 6 — My Helping Hands Plan
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Tell a listener about helping at home. First name three jobs you already do, using words such as sweep, wash and tidy. Then promise three jobs for next week, using will, for example “On Monday I will set the table.” Finish by saying which job you like best and why. Record your plan and listen back for the word will.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Read your three will promises to the tutor with an adult present, and ask whether every sentence used will correctly.

writing.title: Writing 1 — A Paragraph About Your Home
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a short paragraph about your home. Answer all four of these questions inside your paragraph: What kind of home do you live in? How many rooms does it have? Who do you live with? What is your favourite room, and why?
writing.modelText: I live in a small house with my family. Our house has three bedrooms, a kitchen, a living room and a bathroom. My favourite room is the living room, because we all sit together there in the evening.
writing.successCriteria: I answered all four questions, used the words house, room and family, and ended every sentence with a full stop.
writing.support: Say each sentence aloud before you write it, and copy the room words from your word wall.
writing.extension: Add one more sentence that uses because to explain why your favourite room is special.

writing.title: Writing 2 — Your Own Animal Homes Poem
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write four lines like the poem “Homes”. Begin each line with the home and end it with the animal, following the pattern “A / An ______ is a home for a / an ______.” Choose a before a consonant sound and an before a vowel sound, as in “A nest is a home for an owl.” Use two homes from the poem and two new ones of your own, then draw a picture to go with your poem.
writing.modelText:
A nest is a home for a bird.
A hive is a home for a bee.
A den is a home for a fox.
And my house is a home for me.
writing.sentenceStarter: A nest is a home for a bird.
writing.successCriteria: My four lines follow the pattern, every animal really does live in the home I named, and each line starts with a capital letter.
writing.support: Look again at Group 1 of your word list and at the poem before you start.
writing.extension: Add two more lines and make the last words of those two lines rhyme.

writing.title: Writing 3 — Story Response: Helping Hands at Home
writing.promptAndInstructions: Answer two tasks about “Helping Hands at Home”. First write three sentences about jobs you do to help at home, beginning each one with “At home, I ______.” Then draw your favourite room and write two sentences under the picture: one that begins There is and one that begins There are.
writing.modelText:
At home, I make my bed. At home, I sweep the floor after dinner. At home, I set the table for my family.
There is a soft rug in my living room. There are four chairs around our table.
writing.successCriteria: My three job sentences all begin with At home, I, and my two picture sentences use There is and There are correctly.
writing.support: Reread the paragraphs where Amal and Idris tidy the bedroom and the living room.
writing.extension: Write one more sentence saying which of Amal's jobs you would most like to try.

writing.title: Writing 4 — What Is In Each Room
writing.promptAndInstructions: Choose three rooms in your home. For each room write two sentences: one beginning There is, and one beginning There are. Underline the thing you counted in each sentence, and check that every plural word ends in s.
writing.modelText:
In my kitchen there is one big table. In my kitchen there are six chairs.
In my bedroom there is a warm blanket. In my bedroom there are three books on my shelf.
writing.successCriteria: I wrote two sentences for each of three rooms, used There is for one thing and There are for more than one, and underlined every counted thing.
writing.support: Count the objects on your fingers first: one finger means there is, and two or more fingers mean there are.
writing.extension: Add a number word to every sentence, such as one, two, three or six.

writing.title: Writing 5 — Homes Around the World
writing.promptAndInstructions: Compare two homes from Group 5: an adobe house, a stilt house, a cave house or a skyscraper. Write five sentences: one way the two homes are the same, two ways they are different, and two sentences saying why each home suits the place it is built in.
writing.modelText: An adobe house and a cave house are both cool inside. An adobe house is built from mud and dry grass, but a cave house is dug into rock. An adobe house has thick walls, so the hot sun cannot warm the rooms. A cave house is surrounded by rock, so it stays cool in summer and warm in winter. I would rather live in a cave house, because it never gets too hot.
writing.sentenceStarter: An adobe house and a cave house are both
writing.successCriteria: I named two world homes, gave one likeness and two differences, and used because or so to explain why each home suits its place.
writing.support: Reread the text “Different Kinds of Homes” and the Group 5 word meanings before you begin.
writing.extension: Add one sentence about the kind of home you would build in a very rainy place.

writing.title: Writing 6 — My Helping-at-Home Promise
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write six promises about helping at home next week. Use will in five of them and will not in one, and say when you will do each job, for example “On Tuesday I will wash the dishes.” Tick each promise when you have kept it.
writing.modelText: On Monday I will make my bed before school. On Tuesday I will sweep the floor. On Wednesday I will set the table for dinner. On Thursday I will wash the dishes with my brother. On Friday I will tidy my room. I will not leave my toys on the rug.
writing.successCriteria: Every promise uses will or will not, the verb after will has no to and no -ing, and each sentence says when the job will happen.
writing.support: Choose your jobs from Group 4 of the word list before you begin writing.
writing.extension: Write one more sentence saying who will help you and what that person will do.

activities.title: Activity 1 — Match Homes and Who Lives There
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Draw a line from each home to who or what lives in it.
1. house
2. apartment
3. tree house
4. nest
5. hive
6. hole
Choose from: birds, bees, rabbits, a family, city people, children at play.
activities.answerSummary: 1. house → a family. 2. apartment → city people. 3. tree house → children at play. 4. nest → birds. 5. hive → bees. 6. hole → rabbits.

activities.title: Activity 2 — Match Rooms and Activities
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Match each room with what people usually do there.
1. bedroom
2. kitchen
3. bathroom
4. living room
5. dining room
Choose from: cooking, eating, relaxing, sleeping, washing.
activities.answerSummary: 1. bedroom → sleeping. 2. kitchen → cooking. 3. bathroom → washing. 4. living room → relaxing. 5. dining room → eating.

activities.title: Activity 3 — Fill in the Preposition
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Fill in each blank with in, on, under, next to or above.
1. The bed is ______ the bedroom.
2. The book is ______ the table.
3. The shoes are ______ the bed.
4. The chair is ______ the window.
5. The picture is ______ the sofa.
activities.answerSummary: 1. in. 2. on. 3. under. 4. next to. 5. above.

activities.title: Activity 4 — True or False (Home Facts)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write True or False for each sentence. If it is false, write the correct fact underneath.
1. Birds build nests to keep their eggs safe.
2. Bees live in holes in the ground.
3. A tree house is built in the branches of a tree.
4. A hut is usually a big building with many rooms.
activities.answerSummary: 1. True. 2. False: bees live in a hive. 3. True. 4. False: a hut is small, with only one or two rooms.

activities.title: Activity 5 — Room Sort
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Sort these twelve things into the room where each one is usually used: bed, closet, pillow, cooker, kettle, kitchen sink, toothbrush, towel, soap, sofa, rug, bookshelf.
Write four headings — bedroom, kitchen, bathroom and living room — and list three things under each heading.
activities.answerSummary: Bedroom: bed, closet, pillow. Kitchen: cooker, kettle, kitchen sink. Bathroom: toothbrush, towel, soap. Living room: sofa, rug, bookshelf.

activities.title: Activity 6 — Build a Home Map
activities.instructionsAndItems: Draw a simple plan of your home seen from above, and label every room. Then write six sentences about where things are, using in, on, under, next to, between and above once each. Show your map to an adult and read your six sentences aloud.
activities.answerSummary: The plan must show and label at least four rooms. Each of the six sentences uses a different place word and matches the map, for example “The sofa is next to the window.” Check that every sentence starts with a capital letter and ends with a full stop.

assignments.title: Home, Sweet Home learning portfolio
assignments.instructions: Put together two pieces of work. First complete “Writing 1 — A Paragraph About Your Home” and check it against the writing rubric. Then record “Speaking Game — My Home Tour”, walking from room to room and using There is or There are in every room. Submit the paragraph and the recording together.
assignments.sourceFile: Derived from unit writing and speaking tasks

quizzes.question: Which word means a home where bees live and make honey?
quizzes.explanation: Bees build a hive and store their honey inside it. A broom, a ladder and a closet are things at home, not animal homes.

quizzes.question: In “Different Kinds of Homes”, what are adobe houses built from?
quizzes.options: Glass and steel | Mud and dry grass | Wood and rope | Brick and paint
quizzes.explanation: The text says people build adobe houses from mud and dry grass, and that the thick walls keep them cool.

quizzes.question: Which of these things would you find in a bathroom?
quizzes.explanation: A bathroom has a sink and a toilet for washing. A sofa belongs in the living room, a nest is a bird's home, and a ladder is not a bathroom thing.

quizzes.question: The lamp is ____ the two books.
quizzes.explanation: Between means in the middle of two things, so the lamp has one book on each side of it.

quizzes.question: Which sentence is written correctly?
quizzes.options: There is two chairs in the kitchen. | There are two chairs in the kitchen. | There am two chairs in the kitchen. | There be two chairs in the kitchen.
quizzes.correctAnswer: There are two chairs in the kitchen.
quizzes.explanation: Two chairs is more than one thing, so the sentence needs there are.

quizzes.question: Which sentence talks about the future in the right way?
quizzes.options: I will to sweep the floor. | I will sweeping the floor. | I will sweep the floor. | I sweep will the floor.
quizzes.explanation: After will we use the plain verb, with no to and no -ing, and will always comes before the verb.

quizzes.question: In which room do people cook the family meal?
quizzes.options: The bedroom | The bathroom | The kitchen | The living room
quizzes.explanation: The kitchen is the room where food is cooked. People sleep in the bedroom, wash in the bathroom and rest in the living room.

quizzes.question: In “Helping Hands at Home”, what surprise had Father built in the garden?
quizzes.options: A tree house | A stone wall | A flower bed | A new kitchen
quizzes.explanation: Father built a tree house under the big tree, with walls, a small roof and a tiny window.

quizzes.question: What does the word rug mean?
quizzes.options: A soft mat that covers part of the floor. | A basin where you wash with water. | A long, soft seat for two or three people. | An opening in a wall with glass that lets light in.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A soft mat that covers part of the floor.
quizzes.explanation: A rug lies on the floor. The other three options describe a sink, a sofa and a window.

quizzes.question: Which job means putting the plates and cups ready for a meal?
quizzes.options: sweep the floor | wash the dishes | make my bed | set the table
quizzes.explanation: Setting the table means laying out the plates, cups and spoons before people eat.

liveSessions.title: Home, Sweet Home: kinds of homes and the poem

liveSessions.agenda: 5 min welcome; 10 min picture sort of people homes and animal homes; 10 min choral reading of the poem “Homes”; 15 min Speaking 1 in pairs; 5 min reflection
liveSessions.afterSession: Record Speaking 1 — Describe a Kind of Home and upload it.

liveSessions.title: Rooms in a home and the things we keep in them
liveSessions.beforeSession: Learn Groups 2 and 3 of the word list, and name five things in your own kitchen.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min word wall retrieval; 10 min labelled floor plan on the board; 15 min Room Sort in teams; 10 min Speaking 2 in pairs; 5 min exit ticket
liveSessions.afterSession: Finish Activity 5 — Room Sort and check your four headings.

liveSessions.title: Grammar workshop: prepositions of place
liveSessions.beforeSession: Read Grammar Practice 1 and try the five gap sentences on your own.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min hand-in-the-box warm-up; 10 min teacher model with a real box and book; 15 min Where Does It Belong? in pairs; 10 min writing six place sentences; 5 min feedback
liveSessions.afterSession: Complete Activity 3 and begin your home map for Activity 6.

liveSessions.title: Shared reading: Helping Hands at Home
liveSessions.beforeSession: Read “Helping Hands at Home” once at home and underline every job that Amal or Idris does.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min prediction from the title; 15 min shared reading aloud with attention to speech marks; 15 min story questions 12 to 16; 10 min hot-seating Amal and Grandma Hana
liveSessions.afterSession: Answer the three Think and talk questions in your book.

liveSessions.title: Grammar and writing: there is, there are, and homes around the world
liveSessions.beforeSession: Complete Grammar Practices 3 and 4, and reread “Different Kinds of Homes”.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min counting warm-up; 10 min sorting there is against there are; 15 min guided writing of the world homes comparison; 10 min partner proofreading; 5 min revision goal
liveSessions.afterSession: Finish Writing 4 and Writing 5, and check every plural word.

liveSessions.title: Home, Sweet Home: home tour showcase and review
liveSessions.beforeSession: Practise the home tour you planned in Speaking 3 and complete the ten-question checkpoint.

teacherNotes.noteType: AI tutor use and device safety
teacherNotes.note: Every AI tutor task in this unit assumes an adult is present. Never leave a child alone with the device: sit beside them, keep the screen visible, and close the session together. The prompts below come from the source pack, grouped by file. Vocabulary: “What other animals build nests?”, “What is the difference between a living room and a dining room?”, “In which room would I find each of these things?”, “Why do adobe houses have thick walls?” Grammar: “Can you check my grammar answers?”, “When do I use there is and when do I use there are?”, “Can you give me five more sentences with prepositions of place?” Reading and listening: “Can you read the poem with me and help me write a new line?”, “What kinds of homes do people live in around the world?”, “Can you play a listening game about rooms in a home?” Story and writing: “Can you read the story with me and explain any hard words?”, “Can you check my paragraph about my home?”, “Are the animal homes in my poem correct?”

Homes are personal, so let every child choose how much to share. Some children live in one room, some move often, and some share with a large family: accept any home a child describes and never ask for the number of bedrooms as a right answer. Offer the picture bank or the model home plan to any child who would rather describe an imagined home. When marking prepositions, mark the meaning first and the spelling second, and treat a missing there is or there are as the next step rather than as a mistake. The word will is the one pattern most children slip on, so praise the plain verb every time you hear it. The story carries the unit's value: a home is a place where a family helps each other.

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: 1. house → a family. 2. apartment → city people. 3. tree house → children at play. 4. nest → birds. 5. hive → bees. 6. hole → rabbits.
1. bedroom → sleeping. 2. kitchen → cooking. 3. bathroom → washing. 4. living room → relaxing. 5. dining room → eating.
1. in. 2. on. 3. under. 4. next to. 5. above. Accept above or over for number 5 only if the child explains that the picture hangs higher than the sofa.
1. True. 2. False: bees live in a hive, not in a hole. 3. True. 4. False: a hut is small, with only one or two rooms.
1. on. 2. in. 3. under. 4. above. 5. between. Number 4 needs above because the window is higher up the wall, not resting on the bed.
1. is. 2. are. 3. is. 4. are. 5. is. Numbers 2 and 4 take are because chairs and windows are plural.
1. Tomorrow I will sweep the floor. 2. After dinner we will wash the dishes. 3. She will set the table tonight. 4. They will build a tree house in the garden. Do not accept to or -ing after will.
A nest is for a bird, a hive is for a bee, and a hole is for a rabbit. All three pairs are needed for the mark.
A house. The last line reads, “And a house is a home for me.”
A ______ is a home for a ______. Accept the pattern written with any two blanks, or read aloud with two claps for the gaps.
Any two of apartments, houses with a garden, adobe houses and houses on stilts. Two are enough.
A set of rooms that is part of a big building. Do not accept only “a home in a city”.
Mud and dry grass, and the thick mud walls keep the rooms cool. Both halves are needed for the mark.
So that the air can blow underneath the house. Accept “to keep the house cool” only if the air is mentioned.
Theo's father built it, and it is in their garden. Both the builder and the place are needed.
He climbs a ladder. Accept “up a ladder”.
Walls, a roof and a small window. All three parts are needed.
Personal answer. Award the mark when the child gives a clear yes or no and joins it to one sensible reason with because.
Grandma Hana, and she will stay for one whole week. Accept “a week” for the second half.
Any three of the bedrooms, the kitchen, the bathroom and the living room.
Amal made her bed and put her clean clothes in the closet. Idris put his toys from under the bed into the toy box. One job each is enough.
A tree house, with walls, a small roof and a tiny window. Accept two of the three parts.
A place where a family helps each other. Look for the idea that a home is more than walls and a roof.
The house was not ready for a special guest. Accept any one detail: toys under the sofa, dishes in the sink, or dust on the little table.
Personal answer. Award the mark for two real household jobs, each written as a complete sentence beginning At home, I.
She felt very glad. The second mark is for a reason that links her gladness to helping, such as the house looked lovely or the family worked as a team.
hive. A broom, a ladder and a closet are household objects, not animal homes.
Mud and dry grass. The thick mud walls are what keep an adobe house cool.
sink. The sofa belongs in the living room and the nest belongs in a tree.
between. Only between works when there is one thing on each side.
There are two chairs in the kitchen. Two chairs is plural, so the sentence needs are.
A paragraph of four to six sentences that answers all four questions: the kind of home, the number of rooms, who the child lives with, and a favourite room with a reason.
Six sentences about the child's real room, using in, on, under, next to, between and above once each. Each place word must match where the object actually is.
Six sentences across three rooms: one There is and one There are for each room, with the counted thing underlined. Reject There is followed by a plural.
Five promises, four with will and one with will not, each saying when the job will happen. The verb after will must be plain.
Listen for one likeness, two differences and one weather reason joined with so or because. Mark the comparison, not the choice of homes.
The recording must contain six clues, each using a different place word, and the partner's guess after each clue. Mark accuracy of the place words.
Count three present jobs and three will promises, each with a day or time. The closing sentence must name a favourite job and a reason.
Two sentences for each of three rooms, one There is and one There are, with the counted object underlined and every plural ending in s.
Five sentences comparing two Group 5 homes: one likeness, two differences and two reasons that link each home to its place or weather.
Six promises, five with will and one with will not, each naming a job from Group 4 and the day it will be done.
Bedroom: bed, closet, pillow. Kitchen: cooker, kettle, kitchen sink. Bathroom: toothbrush, towel, soap. Living room: sofa, rug, bookshelf.
A labelled plan showing at least four rooms, plus six sentences that each use a different place word and agree with the plan.
I will sweep the floor. Reject to and -ing after will, and reject will placed after the verb.
The kitchen. The other three rooms are for sleeping, washing and resting.
A tree house. Father built it under the big tree in the garden.
A soft mat that covers part of the floor. The other options define a sink, a sofa and a window.
set the table. The other three phrases name sweeping, washing up and bed making.
